course_info.title: Game Programming
course_info.meeting_schedule: Monday, Wednesday, Friday - 4 hours per week

learning_objectives: Compare monolithic vs modular game architectures
Understand the game loop and fixed vs variable timestep
Identify common architectural patterns used in commercial engines

lecture_notes.introduction: Game programming requires disciplined architecture from day one. A poorly structured codebase becomes unmaintainable as scope grows. Today we examine the patterns that professional studios rely on.
key_concepts: The game loop: fixed timestep, variable timestep, and semi-fixed approaches
Separation of update and render phases
Service locator pattern for decoupling subsystems
Command pattern for input handling and replay systems
Observer pattern for event-driven communication between systems
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a minimal game loop in C++ with fixed-timestep update and interpolated rendering

review.value: Physics and game logic update at a constant rate (e.g., 60 Hz) regardless of frame rate, ensuring deterministic simulation
Provides a global access point to a service without coupling code to the concrete implementation, enabling hot-swapping of subsystems

learning_objectives: Contrast inheritance-based hierarchies with composition-based entity systems
Understand data-oriented design principles for cache-friendly component storage
readings: Game Programming Patterns: Component Ch. (Robert Nystrom)

lecture_notes.introduction: The diamond of death in deep inheritance trees has plagued game codebases for decades. ECS offers a composition-first alternative that also aligns with modern CPU cache architecture.
key_concepts: Entity as an ID, not an object: entities are integers indexing into component arrays
Components as plain data structs with no behavior
Systems that iterate over component arrays and perform logic
Archetype-based vs sparse-set storage strategies
Cache-line awareness: why struct-of-arrays beats array-of-structs for iteration
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing a minimal ECS in C++ with PositionComponent, VelocityComponent, and a MovementSystem
setup_requirements: Familiarity with C++ templates and std::vector

review.value: An entity gains behavior by attaching components rather than inheriting from base classes, allowing arbitrary combinations without hierarchy changes
Organize data by how it is accessed (contiguous arrays per component type) rather than by object identity, yielding 5-10x iteration speedups

learning_objectives: Implement finite state machines (FSMs) for character and game state control
Extend FSMs to hierarchical state machines (HSMs) for complex behaviors
Apply the state pattern to avoid sprawling conditional logic
readings: Game Programming Patterns: State Ch. (Robert Nystrom)

lecture_notes.introduction: State machines are the workhorse of game programming. From menu navigation to enemy AI to animation blending, nearly every subsystem can be modeled as a set of states and transitions.
key_concepts: Finite state machines: states, transitions, enter/exit actions
Transition tables vs switch-case vs state pattern implementations
Hierarchical state machines: super-states containing sub-state machines
Pushdown automata for pause/resume state stacking
Concurrent state machines: multiple orthogonal state axes running in parallel
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a player controller FSM with Idle, Running, Jumping, Falling, and WallSliding states, then refactoring into an HSM with Grounded and Airborne super-states
setup_requirements: Sprite sheet for player character animation

review.value: Super-states share common transition logic (e.g., Grounded handles landing) while sub-states handle specific behavior (Idle vs Running)
A stack of states where new states push on top and popping returns to the previous state, ideal for pause menus and conversation interrupts

learning_objectives: Implement the A* pathfinding algorithm from scratch
Understand heuristic selection and its impact on path quality and performance
Apply navigation meshes for 3D environment pathfinding
readings: Amit Patel's A* Tutorial (Red Blob Games)

lecture_notes.introduction: Pathfinding is one of the most visible AI systems in any game. When an NPC walks around an obstacle smoothly, A* is almost certainly involved. Understanding the algorithm deeply lets you tune it for your game's specific constraints.
key_concepts: A* algorithm: open set, closed set, g-cost, h-cost, f-cost
Admissible heuristics: Manhattan, Euclidean, Octile distance
Priority queue implementation with a binary heap for O(log n) operations
Navigation meshes (NavMesh): convex polygon decomposition of walkable surfaces
Path smoothing with funnel algorithm and string-pulling
lecture_notes.demonstration: Implementing A* on a grid, then extending to a NavMesh with the funnel algorithm for smooth paths
setup_requirements: Graph data structure library or custom implementation

review.value: The heuristic h(n) must never overestimate the true cost (admissibility) to guarantee optimal paths; Euclidean distance is admissible for any metric space
A set of convex polygons covering walkable terrain; agents path-find across polygon edges and the funnel algorithm produces smooth trajectories within the corridor

learning_objectives: Implement spatial partitioning structures for efficient collision queries
Understand memory allocation patterns and pool allocators for game objects
Profile and optimize memory access patterns for cache efficiency
readings: Game Programming Patterns: Spatial Partition Ch. (Robert Nystrom)

lecture_notes.introduction: Brute-force collision checking is O(n^2). Spatial partitioning reduces this to near-linear for typical game scenes. Combined with disciplined memory management, these techniques are what separate 30 FPS from 60 FPS.
key_concepts: Quadtrees and octrees: recursive spatial subdivision with dynamic insert/remove
Uniform grids: fixed-cell spatial hashing for dense, evenly distributed objects
Bounding volume hierarchies (BVH): tree of AABBs for complex mesh queries
Pool allocators: pre-allocated fixed-size blocks to avoid malloc/free overhead
Object pools for frequently spawned/despawned entities (bullets, particles, NPCs)
lecture_notes.demonstration: Building a quadtree with visualized subdivision, then profiling collision detection before and after with 10,000 entities
setup_requirements: ECS framework with PositionComponent and ColliderComponent
Profiling tools: Valgrind/Instruments or Tracy Profiler

review.value: Query cost is O(log n) average for point queries; insertion requires splitting nodes when capacity is exceeded, with a configurable max depth to prevent degenerate cases
Pre-allocates a contiguous block of N objects; allocation is O(1) via a free-list pointer, and deallocation returns the slot to the free-list without system calls

class_schedule.topic: Networking Fundamentals and Shader Programming Intro
learning_objectives: Understand client-server vs peer-to-peer game networking models
Implement basic state synchronization with interpolation and prediction
Write a simple vertex and fragment shader for real-time rendering effects
readings: Multiplayer Game Programming Ch. 1-3 (Josh Glazer & Sanjay Madhav)
The Book of Shaders Ch. 1-3 (Patricio Gonzalez Vivo)

lecture_notes.introduction: We close the first unit by bridging two domains that define modern game programming: networking, which connects players, and shaders, which define how the world appears. Both require thinking in terms of latency and throughput.
key_concepts: Client-server authority model: server is the single source of truth
UDP vs TCP for game traffic: why unreliable delivery is often preferable
Client-side prediction and server reconciliation for responsive controls
Vertex shaders: transforming geometry, skinning, and displacement mapping
Fragment shaders: per-pixel lighting, texture sampling, and post-processing effects
lecture_notes.demonstration: Part 1: Sending entity positions over UDP with snapshot interpolation. Part 2: Writing an HLSL shader that combines diffuse lighting with a scrolling texture effect.
setup_requirements: Networking: ENet library or raw BSD sockets
Shaders: GPU with OpenGL 4.5+ or DirectX 11 support

review.value: The client immediately applies input locally, then reconciles when the server confirms or corrects, hiding round-trip latency from the player
Executes once per pixel (fragment) covered by a rasterized triangle; outputs the final color by combining lighting, texture, and material calculations

quizzes.topic: Game Architecture, ECS, and State Machines

questions.question: In an Entity-Component-System architecture, explain why storing components in contiguous arrays (struct-of-arrays layout) is significantly faster for system iteration than storing them as members of entity objects (array-of-structs layout). Reference CPU cache behavior in your answer.
questions.answer: When a system iterates over a single component type (e.g., all PositionComponents), a struct-of-arrays layout stores all Position data contiguously in memory. The CPU prefetcher loads entire cache lines (typically 64 bytes), so sequential access hits the L1/L2 cache nearly every time. In an array-of-structs layout, Position data is interleaved with other component data the system does not need, wasting cache-line space and causing frequent cache misses. For 10,000 entities with 8 component types, SoA can be 5-10x faster because the working set that fits in cache is effectively 8x smaller per system pass.

rubric.key: Explains contiguous memory and cache-line loading
Identifies cache misses from interleaved data in AoS
Provides concrete performance reasoning or example

questions.question: A player character has the following states: Idle, Running, Jumping, DoubleJumping, Falling, WallSliding, and Dashing. Design a hierarchical state machine by grouping these into two super-states: Grounded and Airborne. List which states belong to each super-state and describe one transition that the Grounded super-state handles so its sub-states do not have to duplicate the logic.
questions.answer: Grounded super-state contains: Idle, Running, Dashing. Airborne super-state contains: Jumping, DoubleJumping, Falling, WallSliding. The Grounded super-state handles the 'jump pressed' transition: regardless of whether the character is Idle, Running, or Dashing, pressing jump transitions to the Jumping state in the Airborne super-state. Without the HSM, each Grounded sub-state would need its own identical jump transition, tripling the maintenance burden.

rubric.key: Correct grouping of states into super-states
Identifies a shared transition handled by the super-state
Explains the duplication problem the HSM solves

quizzes.topic: A* Pathfinding, Spatial Partitioning, and Networking

questions.question: Given a grid where the start node S is at (0,0) and the goal G is at (4,3), and there is a wall blocking cells (2,1), (2,2), and (2,3): trace the first 5 nodes expanded by A* using Manhattan distance as the heuristic. Show the f(n) = g(n) + h(n) calculation for each expanded node.
questions.answer: Node 1: (0,0) g=0, h=7, f=7. Node 2: (1,0) g=1, h=6, f=7. Node 3: (1,1) g=2, h=5, f=7. Node 4: (1,2) g=3, h=4, f=7. Node 5: (1,3) g=4, h=3, f=7. A* expands nodes along the corridor south of the wall because f-costs are tied and it follows the path closest to the goal. The wall at column 2 forces the path to go around via row 0 or row 4.

rubric.key: Correct g, h, and f values for each node
Correct expansion order based on f-cost priority

questions.question: Explain client-side prediction and server reconciliation in a networked game. Why is client-side prediction necessary, and what happens when the server's authoritative state disagrees with the client's predicted state?
questions.answer: Client-side prediction is necessary because network round-trip times (50-200ms) would make controls feel unresponsive if the client waited for server confirmation before moving. Instead, the client immediately applies input locally and stores a buffer of recent inputs with sequence numbers. When the server sends back its authoritative state tagged with the last processed input sequence number, the client compares its predicted state at that sequence to the server's state. If they match, no correction is needed. If they disagree, the client snaps to the server state and re-applies all inputs that occurred after the server's confirmed sequence number, effectively replaying unconfirmed inputs on top of the corrected state.

rubric.key: Explains why prediction is needed (latency hiding)
Describes input buffer with sequence numbers
Explains reconciliation: snap to server state and replay unconfirmed inputs

major_assignments.title: ECS-Based Game Engine with A* Pathfinding
major_assignments.description: Build a working 2D game engine using the Entity-Component-System architecture developed in class. The engine must support entity creation/destruction, component attachment, and at least four systems: Movement, Collision (with spatial partitioning), Rendering, and AI Pathfinding (A* on a grid or NavMesh). Demonstrate the engine with a scene containing a player-controlled entity, 50+ AI entities that pathfind toward the player, and static obstacles.
objectives: Demonstrate mastery of ECS architecture with clean separation of data and logic
Implement A* pathfinding that correctly handles obstacles and dynamic re-pathing
Apply spatial partitioning to achieve stable frame rates with 50+ active entities
Write a documented, extensible codebase that could serve as a foundation for future projects
deliverables: C++ source code with CMake build system
Executable binary for the demonstration scene
Performance profiling report showing frame times with and without spatial partitioning
Architecture document (2-3 pages) explaining design decisions

criteria: Entities are IDs, not objects with behavior
Components are plain data with no logic
Systems iterate over component arrays without accessing entity objects
New component types can be added without modifying existing systems
A* produces correct shortest paths around obstacles
Heuristic is admissible and consistent
AI entities re-path when the player moves or obstacles change
Path smoothing or steering is applied for natural movement
Quadtree or grid-based spatial hash is implemented correctly
Collision queries use the spatial structure instead of brute force
Profiling data shows measurable improvement over naive approach
Structure handles dynamic entity movement (insert/remove per frame)
Code compiles without warnings on major compilers
Architecture document clearly explains design tradeoffs
Functions and types are documented with intent, not restated code
Profiling methodology is described and reproducible
Fully functional ECS engine with all four required systems
A* pathfinding is correct, efficient, and handles edge cases
Spatial partitioning provides demonstrable performance improvement
Professional code quality with thorough documentation

major_assignments.title: Networked Multiplayer Prototype with Shader Effects
major_assignments.description: Extend your ECS engine to support basic networked multiplayer and custom shader rendering. Implement a client-server architecture where two clients connect to a host, each controlling an entity. The server is authoritative and clients use prediction with reconciliation. Add at least one custom shader effect (e.g., dynamic lighting, trail effect, or damage flash) rendered via OpenGL or SDL2_gpu.
objectives: Implement client-server networking with authoritative state management
Apply client-side prediction and server reconciliation for responsive controls
Write a vertex and fragment shader that enhances the visual presentation
Integrate networking and rendering cleanly into the existing ECS architecture
deliverables: Updated C++ source with networking and shader systems
Server and client executables demonstrating two-player interaction
Network architecture diagram showing message flow and state synchronization
Shader source files with commented explanations of the rendering math

criteria: Server maintains authoritative game state
Clients send input, not state, to the server
Client-side prediction produces responsive local movement
Server reconciliation corrects mispredictions without visible teleporting
Custom vertex and fragment shader compiles and runs without errors
Shader produces a visually meaningful effect beyond flat color
Shader math is correct and explained in comments
Effect integrates cleanly with the game scene
Networking and rendering are implemented as ECS systems
No hard-coded dependencies between networking and rendering code
Engine remains extensible after additions
Frame rate is stable at 60 FPS with networking active
Network architecture diagram is accurate and clear
Shader source is well-commented with mathematical explanations
README explains build and run instructions for both server and client
Known limitations are documented honestly
Functional networked multiplayer with authoritative server
Client-side prediction and reconciliation work correctly
Custom shader effect is visually polished and technically sound
Clean integration into ECS architecture with professional documentation

weekly_quizzes.description: Timed quizzes assessing understanding of architecture patterns, algorithms, and systems design

programming_labs.description: In-class and take-home coding exercises building incremental engine features

major_assignments.description: Two large projects demonstrating ECS engine development, pathfinding, networking, and shaders

midterm_examination.description: Written and practical exam covering architecture patterns, ECS, state machines, and A* pathfinding

final_project.description: Capstone game built on the student's own engine, integrating all systems from the course

course_policies.attendance: Attendance is mandatory for all lecture and lab sessions. More than 2 unexcused absences will result in a 5% final grade deduction per additional absence.
course_policies.late_submissions: Assignments lose 10% per day late. No submissions accepted more than 3 days late. Extensions require written request at least 48 hours before the deadline.
course_policies.collaboration: You may discuss approaches with classmates but all code must be written individually. Shared code between students will be treated as an academic integrity violation for all parties involved.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All code must be your own work or properly attributed with a comment citing the source. Use of AI code generation tools must be disclosed in your documentation. Unattributed code reuse will result in a zero on the assignment.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Students must have a computer with a C++ compiler (Clang 16+ or MSVC 2022), CMake 3.25+, and a GPU supporting OpenGL 4.5 or DirectX 11. Lab machines are available but personal machines are strongly recommended.
